coredata.dc:description: The fluorescent sensor, especially ratiometric fluorescent sensor, is one of the most important applications for CQDs, which is becoming a research hotspot. Herein, carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen, phosphorus and chlorine (NPCl-CQDs) were synthesized by acid-base neutralization reaction exothermic carbonization method. The as-fabricated NPCl-CQDs could emit blue fluorescence and possess excellent fluorescence properties. Based on the FRET, multifunctional and ratiometric fluorescent sensors for “on-off-on” sequential determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys with good selectivity and high sensitivity were established. The linear range of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys are 0.50–10.18 μM and 15.89–27.76 μM, 0.66–1.46 mM and 1.50–4.20 mM, and 0.01–0.15 μM and 0.15–0.36 μM with the limit of detection of 3.50 nM, 26.38 μM, and 0.96 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the sensors were successfully used to determine riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in tablets, river water, and human urine with the recoveries of 95.2–104.0%, 95.6–102.0%, and 94.8–106.4%, respectively. More importantly, the as-constructed “on-off-on” NPCl-CQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors were applied for detecting riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in HeLa cells with satisfying results. The finding of this study shows the feasibility and effectiveness of the NPCl-CQDs as the available ratiometric fluorescent sensors for the determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in real samples and living cells.

head.ce:title: Ratiometric fluorescent sensors for sequential on-off-on determination of riboflavin, Ag+ and l-cysteine based on NPCl-doped carbon quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Environmental Science, And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Environmental Science, And School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Shanxi University, Taiyuan, 030006, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The fluorescent sensor, especially ratiometric fluorescent sensor, is one of the most important applications for CQDs, which is becoming a research hotspot. Herein, carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen, phosphorus and chlorine (NPCl-CQDs) were synthesized by acid-base neutralization reaction exothermic carbonization method. The as-fabricated NPCl-CQDs could emit blue fluorescence and possess excellent fluorescence properties. Based on the FRET, multifunctional and ratiometric fluorescent sensors for “on-off-on” sequential determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys with good selectivity and high sensitivity were established. The linear range of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys are 0.50–10.18 μM and 15.89–27.76 μM, 0.66–1.46 mM and 1.50–4.20 mM, and 0.01–0.15 μM and 0.15–0.36 μM with the limit of detection of 3.50 nM, 26.38 μM, and 0.96 nM, respectively. Furthermore, the sensors were successfully used to determine riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in tablets, river water, and human urine with the recoveries of 95.2–104.0%, 95.6–102.0%, and 94.8–106.4%, respectively. More importantly, the as-constructed “on-off-on” NPCl-CQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors were applied for detecting riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in HeLa cells with satisfying results. The finding of this study shows the feasibility and effectiveness of the NPCl-CQDs as the available ratiometric fluorescent sensors for the determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in real samples and living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Single emission sensor can be affected by concentration variation of probe, instrument efficiency, background signal, and sophisticated conditions of the real samples, so it is hard to determinate precisely and quantitatively target objects [1,2]. In order to eliminate these adverse factors and obtain accurate detection results, ratiometric fluorescent sensor as an excellent candidate has attracted wide attention. Ratiometric fluorescence is a special detection method based on the ratiometric of fluorescence intensity of different wavelengths [3]. Ratiometric fluorescence sensing can effectively eliminate or reduce background interferences such as temperature, pH, concentration, etc. [4], attributing to a built-in correction in terms of two distinct emission wavelengths. To date, most of the reported ratiometric fluorescent sensors are composed of organic dyes [5], metallic quantum dots [6], metal nanoclusters [7], graphene quantum dots [8], gold nanoparticles [9], or carbon nanomaterials [10]. All results from the reported literature demonstrate that the ratiometric fluorescence sensors possess good selectivity, low background interference and high accuracy [11]. Therefore, it is urgently needed to further enrich and develop the ratiometric fluorescent sensors.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) is a distinguished nanomaterial attributing to its prominent properties such as stable photoluminescence, easy functionalization, excellent biocompatibility, etc. [12–14], which is widely used in fluorescent sensors and biological imaging [15,16]. Many researches have shown that heteroatom doping is an effective method to improve performance and expand the application of CQDs [17,18]. Therefore, we want to fabricate multi-heteroatoms doping CQDs and explore the innovative applications. Recently, a strategy for incorporating CQDs with other materials was developed for the construction of the ratiometric fluorescent sensors [5,19–21]. Li et al. [5] synthesize a ratiometric fluorescent molecularly imprinted sensor for the highly selective and sensitive detection of 4-nitrophenol by using carbon dot and YVO4:Eu3+ as the target fluorophore and the reference fluorophore, respectively. Dong and coworkers [19] construct a far-red FRET fluorescent probe based on carbon dots and N-acetyl-l-cysteine capped gold nanoparticles to detect l-cysteine with satisfying results. Dong et al. [20] design a two color light-emitting nanohybrids (Eu-MOFs/N,S-CDs) by encapsulating blue light-emitting N,S-CDs into red light-emitting Eu-MOFs, which can be used to successfully ratiometric detect water in organic solvents. These ratiometric fluorescent sensors based on CQDs possess good selectivity and high sensitivity, nevertheless, the successful construction of these sensors must rely on CQDs and another material, resulting in the complexity of the building process. Therefore, under the premise of ensuring accuracy and precision, we want to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based solely on CQDs to realize the determination of multi-objects.

Riboflavin (VB2), silver (Ag+) and l-cysteine (Cys) are three substances that are closely related to our lives and environment. Lacking riboflavin in the human body will lead to the obstacle of the biological oxidation and metabolic disturbance [22,23]. Ag+ is an important transition metal, which has become non-negligible environmental pollution originated from large-scale emissions from electronics, pharmaceuticals, photography, and other industries [24,25]. Cys is an essential amino acid that has been linked to growth, angiocardiopathy, Alzheimer’s diseases, and many other diseases [26,27]. Spectrophotometric [28], immunoassay [29,30], capillary electrophoresis (CE) [31–33], and high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [34–36] can be used to detect riboflavin and Cys. For Ag+, atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [37], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry (ICP-MS) [38], colorimetric [39], electrochemical [40], and fluorescent [41,42] methods have been developed in the recent decades years. Although these methods have their advantages, there are some defects, including tedious step, long time-consuming, complicated pre-preparation, expensive instruments, high cost, weak anti-interference ability, and low accuracy, which limit the practical application of these detection methods. Fluorescence assay, especially ratiometric fluorescent method, possesses special advantages such as the background interference self-cancellation, fast response, high sensitivity, superior accuracy and validity, etc [4,11]. As a consequence, we want to construct a ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on CQDs for determining riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in real samples, which will further enrich the application range of CQDs in sensing field.

In this work, carbon quantum dots co-doped with nitrogen, phosphorus, and chlorine (NPCl-CQDs) were synthesized by carbonization method from the acid-base reaction heat with glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), 36.5% hydrochloric acid (HCl), and 85% phosphoric acid (H3PO4) as the reaction precursors. In the absence of an external heat source, the glucose was dehydrated and carbonized within 3 min. The NPCl-CQDs solution has long-term stability and emits bright blue fluorescence. One significant finding is that FRET can occur between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin and that can be broken by Ag+. And then the fluorescence at 530 nm can be recovered by Cys. Therefore, a multifunctional fluorescent sensor for the sequential determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys with good selectivity and high sensitivity was constructed. The sensors have been successfully used for the determination of riboflavin in tablets, Ag+ in river water, and Cys in human urine. More importantly, the sensor was used to detect riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in HeLa cells, demonstrating potential applications in bioanalysis and bioimaging.

Glucose, 1,2-ethylenediamine (EDA), and twenty-two kinds of amino acids were purchased from Sigma Aldrich chemical reagent co. (ST, Louis, USA). 85% (wt%) phosphoric acid (H3PO4), concentrated hydrochloric acid (36.5%, HCl), and other chemical reagents were obtained from Aladdin Reagents Company (Shanghai, China). Six kinds of vitamin B (VB1, VB2, VB3, VB7, VB9, and VB12) were bought from XiYa Reagent (Beijing, China). Other biological reagents were purchased from Solarbio Reagent (Beijing, China). All reagents are analytical grade or chromatography grade. Ultrapure water (≥18.25 MΩ cm) from a Milli-Q system was used throughout the experiment.

The formation process of the NPCl-CQDs is as follows: 0.4 g glucose and 6 mL EDA were added into a 100 mL beaker. After the glucose was completely dissolved, 2 mL H3PO4 and 2 mL HCl were dropwise added into the beaker, and then the dark brown substance was obtained. The crude product was dissolved in ultrapure water to form a brown solution. The solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm for 10 min to obtain the homogeneous solution and lyophilized to acquire the NPCl-CQDs solid powder.

The atomic force microscopy (AFM) image, transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images, and high-resolution transmission electron microscopic (HRTEM) images were acquired on a Veeco Nanoscope Quadrex AFM (Bruker AFM Probes, Camarillo, CA, USA) and a JEOL JEM-2100 transmission electron microscopy (Tokyo, Japan), respectively. The elemental analysis was carried out on a Vario EL cube elemental analyzer (Hanau, Germany). The X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) were obtained from an AXIS ULTRA DLD X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Kratos, Tokyo, Japan). The UV–vis absorption spectra and fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were performed on a Lambda 365 UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer (PerkinElmer, Llantrisant, UK) and a Bruker Tensor II FTIR spectrometer (Bremen, Germany), respectively. The fluorescence spectra were performed on a Varian Cary Eclipse spectrofluorometer (Palo Alto, CA, USA). The nanosecond fluorescence lifetime assays were accomplished on a FLS920 time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) system with 405 nm laser (Edinburgh Instruments, England). Thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) and detailed parameters were the same as those in our previous paper [43].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys by the NPCl-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: NPCl-CQDs powder was dissolved in ultrapure water to form the concentration of 10 mg mL−1 NPCl-CQDs solution. Eighteen kinds of metal ions (Zn2+, Ni2+, Ag+, Hg2+, Al3+, Mg2+, Mn2+, K+, Cd2+, Cu2+, Na+, Pb2+, Fe2+, Ca2+, Ba2+, Fe3+, Cr3+, and Co2+), eighteen kinds of anions (Br−, I−, F−, ClO4 −, ClO3 −, CO3 2−, HS−, HSO3 −, S2O3 2−, HCO3 −, SO3 2−, NO3 −, Cl−, HPO4 2−, NO2 −, H2PO4 −, CH3COO−, and ClO−), twenty-two kinds of amino acids (Valine/Val, Phenylalanine/Phe, Threonine/Thr, Arginine/Arg, Asparagine/Asp, Alanine/Ala, Leucine/Leu, Isoleucine/Ile, Tryptophan/Try, Glycine/Gly, Serine/Ser, l-cysteine/Cys, Tyrosine/Tyr, Histidine/His, Lysine/Lys, Glutamate/Glu, Asparagine/Asn, Glutamine/Gln, Methionine/Met, Proline/Pro, Homocysteine/Hcy, and Glutathione/GSH), sixteen kinds of medicines (Curcumin/Cur, Vitamin C/VC, Acetyl salicylic acid/ASA, Bovine serum albumin/BSA, Morin/MH, Adenosine/AD, Ofloxacin/OF, Adriamycin/Dox, Dopamine hydrochloride/DH, Dopamine/DA, Thiamine/VB1, Riboflavin/VB2, Niacin/VB3, Biotin/VB7, Folic acid/VB9, and Vitamin B12/VB12) and H2O2 were prepared the concentration of 0.01 M for further use. In order to evaluate the selectivity of the NPCl-CQDs to riboflavin, the drugs (32.1 μM) were added into the NPCl-CQDs (0.32 mg mL−1). After that, the fluorescence intensity was measured. To evaluate the interference of various metal ions, anions, amino acids, and drugs to riboflavin, 1.64 mM of metal ions and anions and 0.16 mM of amino acids and drugs were respectively mixed with the NPCl-CQDs (0.49 mg mL−1). After that, the fluorescence intensity was estimated. Riboflavin (15.2 μM) was then added to the above mixture and the fluorescence intensity was estimated. In order to obtain the linear range and detection limit of riboflavin, fluorescence titration of the NPCl-CQDs with riboflavin was performed. A certain amount of riboflavin was added into the NPCl-CQDs solution (0.57 mg mL−1) in the cuvette with a microsyringe. And then the fluorescence intensity of the above mixture solution was measured within 30 s. The data were handled by Origin software. The concentration of riboflavin in real samples was calculated by the linear equation. The above tests were performed at λ ex and λ em of 368 nm and 388–700 nm, respectively.

Metal ions were used to evaluate the selectivity of the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin to Ag+, the concentration of the metal ion is 1.64 mM. In the anti-interference experiment, the concentration of metal ions and Ag+ is 1.64 mM. Amino acids were used to assess the selectivity and anti-interference of the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin&Ag+ to Cys, the concentration of other amino acids and Cys is 0.16 mM. The other experimental procedures are similar to the procedure of the NPCl-CQDs to riboflavin (vide supra). All the tests were obtained at λ ex of 368 nm and λ em of 388–700 nm.

Two tablets of Haiwang VB2 (riboflavin Tablet 1), two tablets of Yuchuan VB2 (riboflavin Tablet 2), and three tablets of Jilaoquan VB2 (riboflavin Tablet 3) were mashed and dissolved into 10 mL of ultrapure water, respectively. They were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 10 min to remove the undissolved substances and gain the supernatant. The environmental water samples were taken from Fenhe in Shanxi of China. The impurities were removed through 0.45 μm aperture filter membrane. Collect 5–10 mL urine samples from healthy volunteers (two men and one woman). The urine samples were centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 15 min and filtered through a 0.45 μm filter membrane [44]. All real samples were stored at 4 °C for further use.

In order to detect the cytotoxicity of the NPCl-CQDs, human cervical cancer (HeLa) cells were inoculated in 96-well cell culture clusters, which were cultured at 37 °C in 5% CO2 for 24 h. Then, the different concentration of the NPCl-CQDs was added into HeLa cells and co-incubated for another 24 h. The culture solution was removed and washed three times with Dulbecco’s Phosphate Buffered Saline (DPBS). 10 μL of CCK-8 and 90 μL of free DMEM were mixed, and then added into each well for 30 min. By SunRise microplate reader (Tecan Austria GmbH, Grodig, Austria), the optical density (OD) of the mixture in each well was measured at 450 nm. The results were calculated by referring our previous work [43].

The HeLa cells were plated on the culture dish and incubated with the NPCl-CQDs (0.50 mg mL−1), the mixture of NPCl-CQDs (0.50 mg mL−1) and riboflavin (25.0 μM), the mixture of NPCl-CQDs (0.50 mg mL−1), riboflavin (25.0 μM), and Ag+ (3.5 mM), and the mixture of NPCl-CQDs (0.60 mg mL−1), riboflavin (25.0 μM), Ag+ (3.5 mM), and Cys (0.5 μM) at 37 °C for 1 h, respectively. Subsequently, HeLa cells were harvested and washed three times by pH 7.4 DPBS. Finally, the images of HeLa cells were obtained from a LSM-880 airy scan super-solution confocal microscope (Zeiss, German).

The detailed preparation process of the NPCl-CQDs by acid-base neutralization reaction exothermic carbonization method is as follow: 6.0 mL of EDA and 0.4 g of glucose were mixed in a beaker. Then 2.0 mL of H3PO4 and 2.0 mL of HCl were added into the beaker to produce the NPCl-CQDs (Scheme 1 ). When H3PO4 was added into the mixed solution of EDA and glucose, it can react violently with EDA to release heat, so glucose was carbonized to form CQDs and N and P were doped into the CQDs. Furthermore, Cl can be doped into the CQDs to fabricate the NPCl-CQDs by the violent reactions between EDA and HCl. Therefore, three heteroatoms (N, P and Cl) were doped into the CQDs during preparation process, EDA, H3PO4 and HCl not only provide enough heat for the carbonization reaction, but also are important dopants. The temperature of the whole reaction process was in the range of 85–90 °C. The reaction lasted about 3 min until exhaustion of the heat from acid-base neutralization reaction. This spontaneous reaction can prepare the NPCl-CQDs without external heating, which is an economical and effective method. The average yield of the NPCl-CQDs is about 58.50% (Table S1) according to the three parallel experiments.

The as-prepared NPCl-CQDs were measured by TEM, HRTEM, AFM and XRD to characterize their morphology and size. As shown in Fig. S1A, the NPCl-CQDs are evenly dispersed on the copper grid with the lattice spacing of 0.21 nm. The particle size range of the NPCl-CQDs is determined to be 2.03–3.92 nm with the average particle size of 3.01 ± 0.2 nm (Fig. S1B). As depicted in Figs. S1C and S1D, the NPCl-CQDs are distributed uniformly on silicon substrate with the average height of ca. 2.80 nm. The XRD pattern of the NPCl-CQDs shows a characteristic peak about 2θ = 20° (Fig. S1E), illustrating that the NPCl-CQDs has an obvious amorphous carbon structure. These results demonstrate that the NPCl-CQDs have the structure of spherical-like and amorphous carbon, possessing excellent dispersing performance in ultrapure water.

The spectral properties including UV–vis absorption spectra, fluorescence spectra, and fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) were investigated. As can be seen from the black line in Fig. 1 A, the NPCl-CQDs have two absorption peaks at 279 nm and 317 nm, which are ascribed to the n→π∗ of CO bond and the excited defect surface states induced by N, P, and Cl heteroatoms [12,45]. The optimal excitation (λ ex, red line) and emission (λ em, blue line) peaks are situated at 368 nm and 453 nm, respectively. And the NPCl-CQDs can emit bright blue fluorescence under 365 nm UV light irradiation. Furthermore, the 3D fluorescence diagram and fluorescence spectra of the NPCl-CQDs under different λ ex were studied and are shown in Fig. 1A and S2A, respectively. The λ em red-shifts from 450 nm to 491 nm when the λ ex of the NPCl-CQDs changes from 300 nm to 430 nm, indicating that the NPCl-CQDs possess the λex-dependent fluorescent behavior. It is a common phenomenon for CQDs [17]. In Fig. 1B, the labelled excitation and emission wavelengths are 368 nm and 453 nm, respectively, which is consistent with Fig. 1A. Quinine sulfate as reference (Fig. S2B), fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of the NPCl-CQDs is calculated as 6.65% (Fig. S2C). Consequently, the NPCl-CQDs is beneficial for fluorescent sensor and cell imaging (vide infra).

Elemental analysis, XPS, and FTIR were used to verify the chemical structure and surface functional groups of the NPCl-CQDs. It can be seen from Table S2A, the NPCl-CQDs are constitutive of C, H, O, N, P, and Cl, and the contents are respectively 20.66%, 7.96%, 27.96%, 21.17%, 9.8%, and 12.45%. The relative numbers of atoms are depicted in Table S2B, demonstrating that the empirical formula of a NPCl-CQD is C11H50N10P2Cl2O11. Furthermore, in the XPS survey scan (Fig. 2 A), there are seven peaks at 98.0 eV, 128.0 eV, 149.0 eV, 194.0 eV, 281.0 eV, 396.0 eV, and 527.0 eV, corresponding to Cl 1s, Cl 2p, P 2p, P 2s, C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. Four peaks at 283.1 eV, 284.7 eV, 288.7 eV, and 286.5 eV in the C 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2B) are corresponding to C–H, CC, O–CO, and C–O respectively [46]. N 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. 2C) shows C–N–C and N–H at 399.5 eV and 400.2 eV, respectively [45]. P 2p XPS spectrum (Fig. 2D) is deconvoluted into two peaks at 130.8 eV and 132.2 eV, representing P 2p states in P–C–H and P–C, respectively [47]. Fig. 2E displays the Cl 2p XPS spectrum which can be dissociated into four peaks at 196.1 eV, 197.3 eV, 198.7 eV, and 202.5 eV, corresponding to P–Cl, Cl–C–H, Cl–C, and Cl–CO, respectively [48–50]. Three peaks at 529.0 eV, 530.8 eV, and 532.5 eV in O 1s XPS spectrum (Fig. S3) correspond to N–O, C–OH, and CO, respectively [45,51]. Moreover, FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2F) shows an absorption peak at 3475 cm−1, representing the stretching vibrations of O–H/N–H [45]. Five distinct absorption peaks at 3008, 2172, 1665, 1318, and 1228 cm−1 respectively correspond to νC-H, νC≡C, νC
O, νC
C, and νC-O. Five characteristic peaks consistent with phosphorus-containing groups at 2501, 1231, 1081, 965, and 897 cm−1, respectively, are associated with νP-OH, νP
O, νC-P-C, νP-O, and νP-OH. Two nitrogen-containing peaks C–H and N–H locate at 1563 and 840 cm−1, respectively. And the important functional group at 515 cm−1 is νC-Cl [48,50,52–54]. These results demonstrate that N, P, and Cl have been doped into the NPCl-CQDs successfully. Some functional groups such as amine, hydroxyl, phosphate, and carboxyl are found on the surface of the NPCl-CQDs, endowing the outstanding properties of the NPCl-CQDs including remarkable fluorescence performance, excellent water solubility, special sensing ability, and good biocompatibility.

For further broaden the applied range of the NPCl-CQDs, the stability of the NPCl-CQDs under salt solution, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), pH, temperature, UV irradiation, xenon lamp irradiation, high temperature environment, and long-term storage were investigated in detail. As shown in Fig. S4A, ultrahigh concentration of KCl solution (4.0 M) has little effect on the fluorescence of the NPCl-CQDs. When high concentration of H2O2 (1.5 M) was applied to the NPCl-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs is still higher than 70% (Fig. S4B), manifesting that the NPCl-CQDs has good oxidation resistance. No significant change of the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs within a wide pH range (3.0–10.0) in Fig. S4C, indicating that the NPCl-CQDs possess pH-independent fluorescent behavior and potential application in cell imaging. When the temperature rises from room temperature to 70 °C, the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs has no significant quenching effect (Fig. S4D). After continuous UV (180 min, Fig. S4E) and xenon lamp (180 min, Fig. S4F) irradiation, the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs does not change significantly, indicating that the anti-photobleaching performance of the NPCl-CQDs is good. TGA was conducted at a range of 25–900 °C, with an increase of 10 °C per minute. As can be seen from Fig. S4G, the weight loss at 135.2–560.6 °C and 560.6–900.0 °C are 67.29% and 5.64%, respectively, which may be the releasing of CO, CO2, NO, and NO2. Almost 25.65% is that phosphorus/chlorine and the incomplete carbonized fraction of the NPCl-CQDs transforms to phosphorus/chlorine and carbon materials. There is no agglomeration phenomenon when the NPCl-CQDs powder was dispersed into ultrapure water, demonstrating that the NPCl-CQDs possess excellent transportation and storage properties. The homogeneity and fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs were almost unchanged, when the NPCl-CQDs solution was stored in refrigerator (4 °C) for about two years. All results amply justify that the NPCl-CQDs possess excellent fluorescence stability, which is conducive to apply the NPCl-CQDs in many areas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: FRET sensing system between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin
ce:section.ce:para:
The most significant finding is that the NPCl-CQDs can be used as an innovative sensor to determine riboflavin. When riboflavin was added into the NPCl-CQDs solution, a new emission peak can be found at 530 nm and is described in Fig. S5A. Therefore, the NPCl-CQDs can be served as a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the determination of riboflavin. The selectivity of the sensor for riboflavin determination was studied. Except riboflavin, eighteen kinds of metal ions (Fig. S5B) and seventeen kinds of anions (Fig. S5C) with the same concentration of 0.32 mM and twenty-two kinds of amino acids (Fig. S5D), fourteen kinds of medicines (Fig. S5E), and H2O2 (Fig. S5E) with the same concentration of 32.1 μM cause negligible change in the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs, demonstrating that the NPCl-CQDs have good selectivity to riboflavin. Besides, the high concentrations of metal ions (1.64 mM, Fig. S6A), anions (1.64 mM, Fig. S6B), amino acids (0.16 mM, Fig. S6C), and medicines (0.16 mM, Fig. S6D) have little effect on the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs, while low concentrations (15.2 μM) of riboflavin can cause the significant changes in the fluorescence spectra and intensities of the NPCl-CQDs. The results demonstrate that the sensor has good anti-interference to the determination of riboflavin. More importantly, the FRET system was constructed between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin, which was confirmed by calculating the distance of donor and receptor and the transfer efficiency. For calculating the distance between donor and receptor, the emission spectrum of the NPCl-CQDs and the absorption spectrum of riboflavin were determined. A distinct overlap is found in Fig. 3 A (green diagonal), indicating that FRET may be occur between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin. Furthermore, according to the energy-transfer theory for a donor-acceptor pair, the distance between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin (R
0 ) can be calculated via the following equation [55]:
R 0

where κ2 is usually assumed to be 2/3, n is the refractive index of the medium assumed to be 1.4, ϕ
D is the quantum yield of the donor, and J(λ) is the spectral overlap integral between the donor emission and the acceptor absorption. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3A, J(λ) is calculated as 1.19 × 10−14 cm3 M−1. The R
0 is estimated to be 1.01 nm, which is fit for the required distance (<10 nm) for constructing FRET system. Furthermore, the transfer efficiency in FRET (η
FRET ) can be calculated through the fluorescence intensity, as follows [56]:
η

where I and I

FRET is increased from 0% to 77.80% with the addition of riboflavin from 0 μM to 36.40 μM. These data sufficiently demonstrate that the FRET system was constructed between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin, and the donor and receptor are respectively the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin.

The sensitivity of the sensor was investigated. The equilibrium time is an important parameter in the titration experiment. When 1.0 μM riboflavin was added into the NPCl-CQDs, the I
530 nm /I
453 nm was increased gradually within 0.0 min–1.5 min and then remained as a stable parameter (Fig. S7A), indicating that 1.5 min is an optimal equilibrium reaction time, which was used in the subsequent experiments. As depicted in Fig. 3B and Fig. S7B, when different concentration of riboflavin (0.50–36.40 μM) was mixed with the NPCl-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity at 453 nm gradually decreases while that at 530 nm gradually increases, illustrating that the energy was transferred from the NPCl-CQDs to riboflavin. The color of the NPCl-CQDs under visible light gradually changes from pale yellow to dark yellow, along with the fluorescence of the NPCl-CQDs varies from blue to bright green (the inset in Fig. 3B) after the addition of riboflavin. The linear range of riboflavin was fitted by Stern-Volmer (SV) equation [56]:
I
530 n m
/

K
S V
[ Q ]
where I
530 nm and I
453 nm are the fluorescence intensity of the mixture of the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin at 530 nm and 453 nm, respectively. Q and K
SV are the concentration of riboflavin and the quenching constant, respectively. As depicted in Fig. 3C–D, there are two linear ranges of 0.50–10.18 μM and 15.89–27.76 μM for riboflavin, corresponding to the linear equations are I
530 nm /I
453 nm = 0.1522c(riboflavin) + 0.3674 (R
2 = 0.9938) and I

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ag+ sensors based on the NPCl-CQDs and the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin
ce:section.ce:para:
The other significative finding is that Ag+ can break the FRET system between the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin, so Ag+ sensor (Ag+(I)) can be constructed based on the mixture of the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin. 1.0 min is the first-rank response time for Ag+ to respond to the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin and was applied to all subsequent experiments, which is shown in Fig. S8A. As can be seen from Fig. S8B, Ag+ can quench the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs, similarly, Ag+ sensor (Ag+(II)) can be formed based on the NPCl-CQDs. For comparison, Ag+(I) and Ag+(II) sensors were investigated in detail. As depicted in Fig. S8C, the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin exhibits good selectivity to Ag+. While as shown in Fig. S8B, the selectivity of the NPCl-CQDs to Ag+ is not good. When Ag+ (0.01–4.20 mM) was mixed with the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin, the fluorescence intensity at 453 nm and 530 nm gradually decreased (Fig. 4 A and Fig. S9A). Fig. 4B–C shows the working curves, which is obtained by plotting the fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔI530 nm/I453 nm) versus Ag+ concentration and fitted by SV equation (vide supra). The linear equations of Ag+(I) sensor are ΔI530 nm/I453 nm = 3.1259c(Ag + ) - 0.8753 (R
2 = 0.9986) and ΔI530 nm/I453 nm = 1.3054c(Ag + ) + 1.9954 (R
2 = 0.9973) with the linear ranges of 0.66–1.46 mM and 1.50–4.20 mM, respectively, and the LOD is as low as 4.1 μM. As displayed in Fig. S9B and Fig. S9C, the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs is quenched by Ag+ (0.01–2.00 mM). The linear range of Ag+(II) sensor is 0.07–0.90 mM with the linear equation of (F0–F)/F0 = 0.5076c(Ag + ) + 0.0795 (R
2 = 0.9991), where F0 and F are the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs without and with Ag+, respectively (Fig. 4D). The LOD of Ag+(II) sensor is 26.38 μM. So, compared with Ag+(II) sensor, Ag+(I) sensor possesses wider linear range, higher sensitivity, and lower detection limit, elucidating that the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin is a better choice than the NPCl-CQDs for Ag+ determination. Finally, as revealed in Table 2 , the constructed Ag+(I) sensor have comparability with other published fluorescence methods for Ag+ determination.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of Cys by the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin&Ag+
ce:section.ce:para:
Another meaningful discovery is that the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin&Ag+ can be used to determine Cys, attributing to the strong interaction between Cys and Ag+ [47]. When Cys (0.00–1.62 μM) was mixed with the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin&Ag+, the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm gradually increased, which is shown in Fig. 5 A, demonstrating that Cys sensor can be constructed based on the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin&Ag+. In addition, compared with other amino acids and CH3COO−, only Cys can recover the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm (Fig. 5B), illustrating that Cys sensor possesses high selectivity. As described in Fig. S10, when 0.20 μM Cys were added into the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin&Ag+, the fluorescence intensity (I
530 nm /I
453 nm ) gradually increased from 0.0 min to 2.5 min and then leveled off after 2.5 min, so the optimum response time is 2.5 min. The linear equations were plotted the fluorescence intensity ratio (ΔI530 nm/I453 nm) versus Cys concentration and fitted by SV equation (vide supra). As depicted in Fig. 5C and D, the linear ranges of Cys are 0.01–0.15 μM and 0.15–0.36 μM with the linear equations of ΔI530 nm/I453 nm = 2.1144c(Cys) + 0.0772 (R
2 = 0.9940) and ΔI530 nm/I453 nm = 0.9009c(Cys) - 0.2606 (R
2 = 0.9920), respectively, and the LOD of Cys is 0.96 nM. The sensor used for the determination of Cys has a wide linear range and low detection limit, which provides a good condition for the application of the sensor. Compared with other reported CQDs-based analytical methods for Cys determination, the constructed Cys sensor possesses higher selectivity, wider linear range, and lower LOD (Table 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The fluorescence mechanism for the “on-off-on” fluorescent sensors
ce:section.ce:para:
An excited state donor transfers energy to an adjacent ground state acceptor through long-range dipole-dipole interactions, which makes the occurrence of FRET process. Several factors such as the distance between donor and acceptor, the overlap extent of absorbance and emission spectra, and the transfer efficiency are important factors for constructing the FRET system. NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin as donor and acceptor, respectively, as mentioned earlier, FRET system was successfully constructed. So, on the one hand, the mechanism of riboflavin determination based on the NPCl-CQDs is attributed to FRET. On the other hand, static quenching and dynamic quenching are two main quenching mechanisms, which are judged by fluorescence lifetime (FLT) of a fluorophore. Static quenching process cannot change the FLT of a fluorophore, while dynamic quenching process can reduce the FLT of a fluorophore. Therefore, the influence of riboflavin on the FLT of the NPCl-CQDs was investigated. Fig. S11A shows the FLT curves of the NPCl-CQDs before and after the introduction of different concentration of riboflavin. The corresponding data (Table S3A) obtained by fitting the fluorescent decay with a bi-exponential function, as follows [62]:
I
( t )
=
A 1
exp
( − t /
τ 1
)
+
A 2
exp
( − t /
τ 2
)
where τ
1 and τ
2 are the time constants of the two radiative decay channels; A
1 and A
2 are the corresponding amplitudes [63]. The average FLT of the NPCl-CQDs is 5.31 ns (χ
2 = 1.243) with the FLTs of τ
1 and τ
2 are respectively 2.47 ns (39.09%) and 7.14 ns (60.91%). When different concentrations of riboflavin were mixed with the NPCl-CQDs, the average FLTs of the NPCl-CQDs are between 4.83 ns and 4.93 ns (Table S3B). The FLTs of the NPCl-CQDs are almost unchanged, which indicates that the fluorescence sensor belongs to the static quenching process. Thus, the fluorescence mechanism of the interaction between the NPCl-CQDs and roboflavin is a synergistic effect of FRET and static quenching (Scheme 2 ).

As displayed in Scheme 2, the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin is a complex. When Ag+ was mixed with the complex, the fluorescence intensity at 453 nm and 530 nm gradually decreased, which may be attributed to three reasons. Ag+ can quench the fluorescence intensity of the NPCl-CQDs (vide supra), weakening of the donor energy and resulting in the decrease of fluorescence intensity at 453 nm, which is the first reason. In addition, Ag+ can combine with riboflavin, leading to the decrease of the acceptor energy and the fluorescence intensity at 530 nm, which is the second reason. The FLTs of the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin without and with different concentration of Ag+ are 5.27 ns and 4.88–5.34 ns (Fig. S11B and Table S3B), which is ascribed to dynamic quenching. In short, the fluorescence mechanism of the interaction between the NPCl-CQDs&roboflavin and Ag+ is a synergistic effect of energy consumption of donor and acceptor and dynamic quenching (Scheme 2).

Cys contains –SH, so a strong interaction between Cys and Ag+ can strip Ag+ from the surface of the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin, resulting in the fluorescence intensity at 453 nm is almost unchanged and that at 530 nm gradually increased (vide infra). But a part of Ag+ cannot separate from the surface of the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin, so only 38.12% fluorescence intensity at 530 nm was recovered. Hence, the determination mechanism of Cys is attributed to the binding interaction between Cys and Ag+ (Scheme 2).

The reliability of the constructed sensors for “on-off-on” sequential determination of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys were evaluated with drugs, environment water samples, and human urine samples, respectively. The recoveries of the method were determined by adding various known concentration of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys, respectively. As shown in Table 4 A, the riboflavin contents in Tablet 1, Tablet 2, and Tablet 3 determined by the sensor are 0.64, 0.89, and 0.61 μM, respectively, with the relative standard deviations (RSDs) are between 2.4% and 7.3% (n = 3). The recoveries of riboflavin in the tested tablets are 95.2–104.0% with RSDs are between 1.2% and 3.8% (n = 3) (Table 4B). Ag+ contents in Taiyuan Fenhe water 1, 2, and 3 were not detected. In Table 4C, the quantitative spike recoveries for the determination of Ag+ in Fenhe water samples are of 95.6%–102.0% with the RSDs <2.9% (n = 3), indicating that the sensor is suitable for the analysis of real water samples. The content of Cys in the human urine samples of the volunteers was measured, including two healthy men and one healthy woman. The results were 23.93 nM, 31.44 nM, and 11.40 nM, respectively, and the RSDs ranged from 4.7% to 5.6% (Table 4D). As depicted by Table 4E, the human urine samples recoveries are in the range of 94.8–106.4% and the RSDs are between 1.8% and 4.7% (n = 3). Consequently, the NPCl-CQDs is promising in determining of the content of riboflavin in drug samples, Ag+ in environmental water samples, and Cys in human urine samples.

Based on the excellent optical properties and fluorescence sensors of the NPCl-CQDs, the biological application of the NPCl-CQDs was explored in detail. Low cytotoxicity is the important property for CQDs, which can guarantee CQDs to be applied in cell imaging. CCK-8 method was used to estimate the viability of HeLa cells, and HeLa cells were cultured by incubating with 0, 5, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 150, 200, and 300 μg mL−1 of the NPCl-CQDs for 24 h. As shown in Fig. S12, when the concentration of the NPCl-CQDs is 300 μg mL−1, the viability of HeLa is still higher than 82%, demonstrating that the NPCl-CQDs is low cytotoxicity and can be used in cell imaging.

HeLa cell, as a model cell, was used to investigate the intercellular detection of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys. As displayed in Fig. 6 , the cellular uptakes of the NPCl-CQDs were observed via laser scanning confocal microscopy (LSCM). The stained cells can emit most intensely blue and green emissions when they were stimulated by 405 and 488 nm lasers, respectively. When the NPCl-CQDs were incubated with HeLa cells, only blue fluorescence is emitted in cells. When the NPCl-CQDs&riboflavin was mixed with HeLa cells, the blue fluorescence is gradually weakened, but the green fluorescence can be found in cells. When Ag+ entered into the nutrient solutions with the NPCl-CQDs and riboflavin, the blue and green fluorescence in cells were gradually weakened. Adding Cys, the blue fluorescence emitted from cells is almost unchanged while the green fluorescence emitted from cells was gradually strengthened. The LSCM images of HeLa cells cultured with NPCl-CQDs, NPCl-CQDs + riboflavin, NPCl-CQDs + riboflavin + Ag+, and NPCl-CQDs + riboflavin + Ag+ + Cys possess normal morphology, indicating that NPCl-CQDs, riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys are biocompatible and minimum cytotoxicity to cells. The above results indicate that the NPCl-CQDs can be used as highly efficient fluorescence sensors for “on-off-on” detection of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in living cells.

In summary, glucose, EDA, H3PO4, and HCl are respectively served as carbon source, N dopant, P dopant, and Cl dopant, were used to prepare the NPCl-CQDs. All characterizations demonstrate that the NPCl-CQDs possess excellent water solubility, strong fluorescence stability, and good biocompatibility. Based on FRET and static quenching, the ratiometric fluorescent sensor for riboflavin determination was constructed. Ag+ can break the FRET process, so a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for the determination of Ag+ was established. Furthermore, the interaction between Cys and Ag+ make the fluorescence at 530 nm recover to build a ratiometric fluorescent sensor for Cys determination. The multifunctional and ratiometric fluorescent sensors show good selectivity and high sensitivity towards riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys with the LOD are as low as 3.50 nM, 26.38 μM, and 0.96 nM, respectively. Comparing with other detection methods, the NPCl-CQDs-based ratiometric fluorescent sensors provide better selectivity and higher sensitivity and can be successfully used for riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys determination in drug samples, environmental water samples, and human urine samples, respectively. More importantly, the sensors exhibit superior ability in the detection of riboflavin, Ag+, and Cys in live cells. Considering easy operation and high accuracy of these fluorescent sensors, the NPCl-CQDs are promising in applying in supervision of drug, water quality, body fluid, and viable cells.
